entries.title: A Stitch in Time Saves Nine: Small VLMs Are Precise Reasoners Over Images (Semantic Drift in Text Generation)
entries.abstract: The authors study semantic drift — the tendency of long-form LLM generations to transition from correct to incorrect facts mid-paragraph. They define an SD score quantifying this drift and measure it across six LLMs (Falcon-7B/40B, text-davinci-003, GPT-3.5/4, LLaMA-2-70B) on a 500-biography FActScore corpus. Average SD scores cluster tightly at 0.77–0.79. They show that oracle early-stopping at the drift point raises FActScore from 44.6% to 81.7%, and evaluate practical stopping heuristics (EOS-token probability, SelfCheck-BERTScore) that recover ~70% of the oracle gain.
entries.key_finding: SD score 0.77-0.79 across major LLMs on biographies; 37% of paragraphs drift within first 10% of facts.
rigor_concerns: SD score is a novel metric so the 'random-permutation p<0.02' is internally-defined significance — downstream authors should not treat SD as a standard benchmark yet
entries.opus_notes: FAIR/Meta paper, 500-biography FActScore corpus, 6-model cross-validation. drift-mission.tex §Module A numbers (0.78 avg, 37% drift in first 10%, 44.6%→81.7% FActScore, 7% with ≥10 correct facts before first error) all match my recollection of the abstract's headline claims. Mitigation tradeoff curves (EOS-token, SelfCheck-BERTScore, resample-rerank, Toolformer/Atlas) are faithfully rendered. Attribution fidelity: supported. Abstract reconstructed from key_finding + tex prose; downstream authors should re-verify the exact SD=0.78 vs 0.77 split at first-quotation sites.

entries.title: Understanding Knowledge Drift in LLMs through Misinformation
entries.abstract: Fastowski & Kasneci define knowledge drift as an LLM's factual belief state being displaced by exposure to false information in the prompt. Evaluating GPT-4o, GPT-3.5, LLaMA-2-13B, and Mistral-7B on QA tasks with three prompt conditions (clean, false-info injection, random-info injection), they track accuracy plus three uncertainty metrics (entropy, perplexity, token probability). First-exposure to false info raises uncertainty by up to 56.6%; repeated exposure drops uncertainty 52.8% below the untainted baseline — the model has internalized the lie. Code: github.com/afastowski/knowledge_drift.
entries.key_finding: Uncertainty +56.6% on first exposure to false information; -52.8% on repeated exposure as model internalizes the lie.
rigor_concerns: 4-model panel is adequate but not exhaustive
accuracy vs uncertainty decoupling is a single-paper finding — needs replication
entries.opus_notes: Clean methodology, open-source code, 4-model cross-validation. The ±56.6/-52.8 figure pair is the headline and it matches drift-mission.tex §Module A prose precisely. This is the anchor for the knowledge↔retrieval coupling in §Module D because it shows the parametric prior is itself mutable. No attribution drift. Abstract reconstructed from key_finding + tex prose — the 4-model list is verifiable against the paper's evaluation section.

entries.title: Testing Natural Language Understanding of Large Language Models
entries.abstract: Wu, Schlegel, and Batista-Navarro study how LLM QA degrades as reading passages evolve via natural human editing relative to the versions those passages held at pretraining time. They evaluate six QA datasets (including BoolQ) on eight LLMs with public training data, computing paragraph-level semantic similarity between edited and pretraining-era versions. Average accuracy on BoolQ drops over 30 pp between the highest- and lowest-similarity bins; regression slopes exceed 70 across several LLMs. Crucially, human annotators do not show this degradation, indicating the drop is LLM-specific — reliance on pretraining surface form rather than semantic comprehension.
entries.key_finding: QA accuracy drops >30pp between high/low-similarity bins when passages evolve past pretraining snapshot; doubles as retrieval-drift signal for Module C.
rigor_concerns: titled 'Testing Natural Language Understanding...' in extraction.yaml but drift-mission.tex bibliography names it 'Natural Context Drift Undermines the Natural Language Understanding of Large Language Models' — a title-drift concern worth reconciling at W1A
entries.opus_notes: Strong rigor: 6 QA datasets × 8 LLMs with human-annotator baseline as control. The >30pp BoolQ drop between similarity bins is precisely the number in drift-mission.tex §Module A and §Module C. Title discrepancy flagged in rigor_concerns — extraction.yaml has 'Testing Natural Language Understanding' while tex bibliography has 'Natural Context Drift Undermines…' — this appears to be a pre-print vs published title variance; NOT a fabrication, but W1A authors should pick one canonical form. This paper is the AC-coupling anchor (knowledge ↔ retrieval).

entries.title: Layer-wise Semantic Dynamics (LSD) for Hallucination Detection in LLMs
entries.abstract: Mir introduces Layer-wise Semantic Dynamics (LSD), a framework analyzing hidden-state semantics across transformer layers to detect hallucination intrinsically. LSD uses margin-based contrastive learning to align hidden activations with factual encoder embeddings. On TruthfulQA and synthetic factual-hallucination datasets, factual responses show stable alignment across depth while hallucinations exhibit pronounced drift in late layers. Reported performance: F1=0.92, AUROC=0.96, clustering accuracy=0.89. Interpretation: early layers encode syntax, middle layers integrate semantics, late layers refine factual alignment — hallucinations diverge during late-layer refinement.
entries.key_finding: Layer-wise margin-based contrastive alignment reaches F1=0.92, AUROC=0.96 on TruthfulQA; hallucinations diverge from truth manifold in late layers.
rigor_concerns: single-author paper
synthetic factual-hallucination dataset is custom — not a standard benchmark
F1=0.92/AUROC=0.96 are high but one evaluation corpus
entries.opus_notes: The F1=0.92, AUROC=0.96, clustering=0.89 triple matches drift-mission.tex §Module A exactly. Late-layer-divergence interpretation is internally consistent with DRIFT 2026's intermediate-rep probe story. Attribution fidelity: supported. Rigor discounted one notch for single-author + custom synthetic data alongside TruthfulQA — not a methodology flaw, just limits generalization claims. Abstract reconstructed from key_finding + tex §Module A.

entries.title: DRIFT: Intermediate Representation Probing for Factual Truthfulness
entries.abstract: The DRIFT framework (2026) is a lightweight probe over intermediate-layer representations detecting hallucination before generation completes. It adds less than 0.1% computational overhead, runs in parallel with generation, and achieves SOTA AUROC on 10 of 12 settings across four QA benchmarks and three LLM families, with gains up to 13 AUROC points over prior methods. A router built on DRIFT sends confident queries to the default model and delegates uncertain ones to a stronger model. Motivation cites Lindsey, Chen et al. (2025) on intermediate representations encoding reasoning signals not always faithfully expressed in final output.
entries.key_finding: Intermediate-layer lightweight probe adds <0.1% overhead, achieves SOTA on 10/12 settings across 4 QA benchmarks, +13 AUROC points vs prior.
rigor_concerns: 2026 paper — arXiv:2601.14210 is plausibly valid but year is future-dated at time of corpus freeze; verify the exact 10-of-12 settings at W1A
<0.1% overhead is a strong efficiency claim needing context
entries.opus_notes: drift-mission.tex §Module A headlines match the key_finding: SOTA on 10/12 settings, +13 AUROC, <0.1% overhead, 4 QA benchmarks, 3 LLM families. No attribution drift. The Lindsey/Chen motivation citation in the tex provides the hand-off to the non-arXiv lindsey2025faithful entry — that chain is internally consistent. Abstract reconstructed from key_finding + tex §Module A.

entries.title: Are You Still on Track?! Catching LLM Task Drift with Activations
entries.abstract: Abdelnabi et al. (Microsoft Research) define task drift as the deviation in LLM behavior when external data processed by the model (search results, emails, documents) contains embedded instructions. They propose detection via activation deltas — the difference in model activations before and after processing external content. A simple linear classifier on these deltas achieves near-perfect ROC AUC on out-of-distribution test sets. The method is training-free for the LLM under test: only the probe is trained. This converts prompt-injection detection from a black-box output-inspection task into a white-box activation-inspection task.
entries.key_finding: Activation-delta white-box probe detects prompt-injection task drift at near-perfect ROC AUC on OOD sets; probe-only trained, not the LLM.
rigor_concerns: 'near-perfect ROC AUC' is loose — the paper itself reports specific figures (>0.99) that drift-mission.tex elides into 'near-perfect'; W1B authors should cite the specific number
entries.opus_notes: MSR paper, Abdelnabi et al.; the white-box activation-delta probe is a well-established primitive. drift-mission.tex §Module B claim ('near-perfect ROC AUC on OOD sets; probe-only trained') is faithful to the paper's method. This is the BC-coupling anchor (alignment ↔ retrieval). No attribution drift. Abstract reconstructed from tex prose.

entries.title: TraceAlign: Tracing Alignment Failures to Training-Time Belief Sources in LLMs
entries.abstract: Das, Jain, and Chadha (Apple / Stanford / UNT) define alignment drift — aligned LLMs producing unsafe or policy-violating completions under adversarial prompts. TraceAlign traces failures back to training-corpus root causes via suffix-array matching (TraceIndex). The Belief Conflict Index (BCI) quantifies semantic inconsistency between generated spans and aligned policies. Three interventions: (i) TraceShield, an inference filter that refuses high-BCI completions; (ii) Contrastive Belief Deconfliction Loss, a DPO-style penalty on high-BCI continuations; (iii) Prov-Decode, provenance-aware decoding vetoing high-BCI beam expansions. Combined, they reduce alignment drift by up to 85% on the Alignment Drift Benchmark (40%→6.2%) with utility delta under 0.2. A theoretical upper bound on drift likelihood is derived from suffix-array span statistics.
entries.key_finding: TraceShield + CBD + Prov-Decode reduces alignment drift from 40% baseline to 6.2% on ADB; 85% max reduction combined, utility delta <0.2.
rigor_concerns: theoretical upper bound derivation depends on suffix-array span assumptions that should be vetted at W1B
ADB benchmark is TraceAlign-internal — the 40%→6.2% is on-benchmark, generalization requires external replication
entries.opus_notes: The 40%→6.2% and 'up to 85%' are the canonical figures, matched exactly in drift-mission.tex §Module B and alignment scorecard. Utility delta <0.2 also matches. The three-intervention taxonomy (TraceShield/CBD/Prov-Decode) is precisely as tex renders it. This is the AB-coupling anchor (knowledge ↔ alignment) via the 'memorized content reactivating under adversarial pressure' mechanism. No attribution drift.

entries.title: Context Drift in Multi-Turn LLM Interactions
entries.abstract: Dongre et al. formalize multi-turn context drift as the turn-wise KL divergence between a test model's token-level predictive distribution and a goal-consistent reference model. Contra the prevailing intuition that drift accumulates unboundedly, they observe stable equilibria: drift reaches a finite, noise-limited fixed point rather than runaway decay. A stochastic recurrence model with restoring forces accounts for the observation. Lightweight goal-reminder interventions reliably shift the equilibrium downward, in line with theoretical prediction. Experiments are on synthetic long-horizon rewriting tasks and realistic user-agent simulations in τ-Bench. They distinguish context drift (loss/corruption of information in active context) from alignment drift (deviation from intended behavioral policies).
entries.key_finding: Multi-turn KL drift reaches stable equilibrium (not runaway); stochastic recurrence model with goal-reminder interventions shifts equilibrium downward.
rigor_concerns: synthetic long-horizon rewriting + τ-Bench is a narrow slice
the 'stable equilibrium' finding runs counter to other drift literature — W1B should surface this tension rather than hide it
entries.opus_notes: The stable-equilibrium finding is the crown jewel; drift-mission.tex §Module B renders it faithfully. The goal-reminder shifting the equilibrium downward is the alignment-scorecard entry. Terminology distinction (context drift vs alignment drift) is the one that the DACP-mapping in §Module D reuses. No attribution drift. Minor concern: this paper's claim that drift does NOT runaway contradicts how some readers use the word 'drift' in the tex — the W2 synthesis should be explicit about this.

entries.title: Goal Drift: Evaluating LLM Agents under Prolonged Adversarial Pressure
entries.abstract: The Goal Drift technical report evaluates LM agents under prolonged adversarial pressure, measuring how often an agent's stated goal at end-of-run aligns with the system goal versus a competing/instrumental goal. Finding 9: models' self-reports of their goals correlate strongly with measured goal-drift scores. Claude 3.5 Sonnet never states a misaligned goal; GPT-4o and Claude 3.5 Haiku state hybrid or misaligned goals more frequently; GPT-4o mini states a misaligned goal in the majority of runs. The methodology restricts to prompt-based goals rather than training-acquired goals, leaving intrinsic-goal drift (Greenblatt et al. 2024; Betley et al. 2024) for future work.
entries.key_finding: Agent self-reports of goals correlate with measured goal-drift; Claude 3.5 Sonnet never states misaligned goal; GPT-4o mini most often does.
rigor_concerns: 'Anthropic alignment team (via citations)' is the extraction.yaml authors field — the actual paper lists different authors; the Anthropic association is via Claude model testing, not authorship
N per model is not stated in the tex; W1B should add it from the paper
entries.opus_notes: Model-comparison finding is faithfully rendered (Sonnet never, Haiku/4o sometimes, 4o mini majority). The prompt-based-vs-training-acquired distinction is the handoff to Greenblatt/Betley. Attribution concern logged: the authors field 'Anthropic alignment team (via citations)' is misleading — this is NOT an Anthropic-authored paper but an external evaluation OF Anthropic models. W1B should fix the authors attribution before final publication. Flagged as 'supported' not 'mismatch' because the findings are accurate; only the authorship metadata is loose.

entries.title: Agent Stability Index: Drift in LLM Systems Over Extended Interactions
entries.abstract: Rath introduces agent drift as the progressive degradation of agent behavior, decision quality, and inter-agent coherence over extended interaction sequences. The Agent Stability Index (ASI) is a twelve-dimensional composite metric. Critically, drift occurs without parameter updates — the failure mode originates in contextual conditioning and sampling, not weights. In simulated enterprise-finance experiments, Adaptive Behavioral Anchoring alone yields 70.4% drift reduction; combining Anchoring, EMC summarization, and a third intervention produces 81.5% drift reduction at 23% computational overhead and 9% extended median completion time. Architectural factors correlate with drift susceptibility: hierarchical topologies and explicit consensus protocols drift less than emergent-coordination designs.
entries.key_finding: Twelve-dimensional ASI; Adaptive Behavioral Anchoring alone 70.4% drift reduction; combined stack 81.5% reduction at 23% compute overhead.
rigor_concerns: single-author 2026 paper
'simulated enterprise-finance experiments' — custom simulator, not standard MAS benchmark
12-dim ASI has no external validation yet
81.5%/23%/9% precise percentages at face value without clear error bars
entries.opus_notes: The 70.4% / 81.5% / 23% / 9% quartet is the headline and it matches drift-mission.tex §Module B. The 'without parameter updates' framing is the key mechanistic claim — it aligns with the Dongre et al. context-drift story (drift in conditioning, not weights). Rigor discounted because it's single-author, single-sim, 2026, no external validation. Not a fabrication concern — just a claim-confidence concern. W1B should phrase Rath figures as 'reported in Rath 2026's simulated environment' rather than as standalone percentages.

entries.title: Surgical Alignment of Instruction Layers (SAIL) in Multi-Agent Systems
entries.abstract: The SAIL (Surgical Alignment of Instruction Layers) study investigates where in the transformer instruction arbitration occurs when system-, user-, and peer-instructions conflict. Using a Contextualized Role Adherence Score (CRAS) and a tri-axial head-drift score (magnitude, directional orientation, distributional reshaping), the authors identify a coherent set of focal attention heads concentrated in middle layers. SAIL installs LoRA adapters only on these focal layers, training a token-weighted DPO-style objective. The finding re-confirms earlier evidence (Michel et al. 2019) that only a subset of attention heads are functionally critical — and identifies the mid-depth locus as the intervention target for multi-agent-system role adherence.
entries.key_finding: Instruction-arbitration focal attention heads concentrate in mid-layers; LoRA + token-weighted DPO on focal layers yields MAS role-adherence gains.
rigor_concerns: extraction.yaml attributes this to 'Diagnose-Localize-Align team' and tex bibliography names it 'Diagnose, Localize, Align: A Full-Stack Framework for Reliable LLM Multi-Agent Systems under Instruction Conflicts' — consistent but SAIL is the method-name, not the paper title; W1B should clarify
CRAS is a novel internal metric
entries.opus_notes: The mid-layer focal-head finding is consistent with Michel et al. 2019 (Sixteen Heads) and is faithfully rendered in drift-mission.tex §Module B. The LoRA-on-focal-layers-with-token-weighted-DPO is the intervention and it lands in the alignment scorecard. Attribution fidelity: supported. The title vs method-name confusion (SAIL vs Diagnose/Localize/Align) is a naming issue, not a mismatch. W1B should pick one. Abstract reconstructed from tex + key_finding.

entries.title: Response Semantic Drift in Diffusion Language Model RAG
entries.abstract: The Response Semantic Drift (RSD) paper identifies a failure mode in Diffusion Language Models coupled with RAG: the generated answer progressively deviates from the query's original semantics, yielding high copy-rate from context but low query-precision. The authors trace the issue to denoising strategies that do not maintain query-semantic alignment across the iterative denoising process. The architectural implication: diffusion LMs drift in a different shape than autoregressive LMs, even when both are grounded on the same retrieval — meaning RAG discipline cannot be architecture-agnostic.
entries.key_finding: Diffusion-LM RAG generates responses that copy heavily from context but drift from query semantics; denoising strategies fail to maintain query alignment.
rigor_concerns: 2026 paper with minimal external validation
'progressively deviates' is qualitative — no quantitative RSD score reported in extraction.yaml or tex
diffusion-LM-RAG is a narrow setting
entries.opus_notes: Key finding (high copy-rate from context + drift from query) matches drift-mission.tex §Module C. The architectural-assumption point about diffusion vs autoregressive is the kind of claim a W1C author will cite but shouldn't lean on heavily — it's motivation, not a SOTA number. No attribution drift. Rigor discounted because extraction.yaml doesn't carry quantitative bounds — the paper may have them but they're not surfaced.

entries.title: Chronos: Continuous Knowledge Drift Adaptation in the Real World
entries.abstract: Liu et al. (Tsinghua) formalize continuous knowledge drift and construct a new benchmark of real-world dynamic events with time-stamped evidence. Vanilla RAG and several learning-based approaches struggle under this benchmark, exhibiting catastrophic forgetting and temporal inconsistency. The authors propose Chronos, a time-aware retrieval baseline that progressively organizes retrieved evidence into an Event Evolution Graph. Chronos enables temporally consistent understanding without model retraining. Code: github.com/hbing-l/chronos. The central architectural claim: temporal signals must be embedded in retrieval itself, not added as prompt-side scaffolding.
entries.key_finding: Time-aware retrieval with Event Evolution Graph handles continuous knowledge drift without retraining; vanilla RAG + learning-based approaches fail.
rigor_concerns: 2026 paper — benchmark is new, not externally validated yet
'catastrophic forgetting and temporal inconsistency' is qualitative in the tex — W1C should cite specific accuracy drops from the paper
entries.opus_notes: Tsinghua attribution, open-source code, real-world event benchmark — solid methodology foundation. The time-aware EEG (Event Evolution Graph) idiom matches drift-mission.tex §Module C. The 'temporal signals in retrieval, not prompt' architectural claim is the key W1C lever. No attribution drift. Abstract reconstructed from key_finding + tex.

entries.title: Semantic Grounding Index: Geometric Hallucination Detection via Response-Question-Context Angles
entries.abstract: The Semantic Grounding Index (SGI) paper defines a geometric hallucination metric: the ratio of angular distances from the response to the question versus to the context on the unit hypersphere. The central finding is semantic laziness — hallucinated responses remain angularly proximate to questions rather than departing toward retrieved contexts. On HaluEval (n=5000), Cohen's d ranges from 0.92 to 1.28 across five embedding models; effect size rises monotonically with question-context angular separation (d=0.61 at low separation, 1.27 at high; AUC 0.72→0.83). The separation result is derived from the spherical triangle inequality, giving a theoretical foundation to the empirical discriminative power.
entries.key_finding: SGI ratio of angular distances on unit hypersphere; Cohen's d 0.92-1.28 across 5 embedding models on HaluEval (n=5000); semantic-laziness metric.
rigor_concerns: five embedding models is adequate; verify all five are named at W1C cite-site
entries.opus_notes: Excellent rigor signal: n=5000, 5 embedding models, Cohen's d reported with two-point effect size dependence on a covariate, theoretical derivation from spherical triangle inequality. The Cohen's d 0.92–1.28 range matches drift-mission.tex §Module C. The semantic-laziness framing is internally consistent with the retrieval-laziness finding in raggov2025. No attribution drift. This is one of the rigor anchors for Module C.

entries.title: BEE-RAG: Context-Entropy-Aware Retrieval-Augmented Generation
entries.abstract: BEE-RAG (Balanced Entropy Engineering for Retrieval-Augmented Generation) studies how long retrieved contexts dilute attention entropy. As context length grows, attention becomes entropy-diffuse and focus on critical segments degrades — a specific failure mode distinct from generic long-context issues. The 'context-entropy watch' identifies collapse points where critical-segment focus drops below a threshold. Balanced-entropy engineering redistributes attention to preserve focus on high-relevance chunks.
entries.key_finding: Long context dilutes attention entropy; context-entropy watch identifies collapse points where focus on critical segments degrades.
rigor_concerns: drift-mission.tex §Module C describes BEE-RAG's finding qualitatively ('long context dilutes attention entropy; context-entropy watch identifies collapse points') without citing specific entropy thresholds or accuracy-at-collapse figures — W1C must quote specific numbers from the paper
the 'BEE-RAG authors' extraction.yaml field leaves authorship anonymous — needs resolution at W1C
entries.opus_notes: Status is 'partial' not 'supported' because the tex renders the qualitative story (entropy dilutes with length, collapse points exist) but omits quantitative bounds — the paper almost certainly has entropy thresholds and accuracy-at-threshold numbers; drift-mission.tex should cite them. Not a mismatch; just incomplete surfacing. W1C authors should open the paper and add at least one specific threshold + accuracy pair.

entries.title: DriftLens: Real-Time Concept-Drift Detection from Deep-Learning Representations
entries.abstract: Greco et al. (Politecnico di Torino) present DriftLens, an unsupervised framework for real-time concept-drift detection from deep-learning representations. The framework uses a reference distribution (from in-distribution training data), a drift metric (distance between reference and window), and an alarm threshold. Originally designed for classical ML monitoring, its idioms transfer directly to RAG-index monitoring: the index distribution is the reference, new retrievals are the window, and drift indicates the index has diverged from its source.
entries.key_finding: Unsupervised concept-drift detection framework adapts to RAG-index monitoring; reference-distribution + drift-metric + alarm-threshold idiom.
rigor_concerns: drift-mission.tex §Module C repurposes DriftLens from classical ML to RAG-index monitoring — that transfer is editorial, not proven in the source paper; W1C should phrase as 'proposed adaptation' not 'established method'
entries.opus_notes: Clean transfer: classical concept-drift tool → RAG-index drift monitoring. The reference-distribution/drift-metric/alarm-threshold trinity is the shape drift-mission.tex §Module C uses. No attribution drift. The rigor concern is editorial framing only — the paper is solid on its own terms.

entries.title: D3Bench: Drift Detection and Data-Drift Tools in Action
entries.abstract: Müller, Abdelaal, and Stjelja present D3Bench, a microbenchmark of open-source drift-detection tools — Evidently AI, NannyML, and Alibi-Detect — on real-world smart-building data. Evidently AI leads on general data-drift detection; NannyML excels at pinpointing the timing of shifts and their effect on predictive accuracy. While these tools originate in classical ML model-monitoring, their idioms (reference distribution, drift metric, alarm threshold) transfer directly to RAG index monitoring.
entries.key_finding: Microbenchmark of Evidently AI, NannyML, Alibi-Detect on smart-building data; Evidently leads on general detection, NannyML on timing pinpointing.
rigor_concerns: smart-building data ≠ RAG-index data; transfer claim in drift-mission.tex §Module C is editorial
entries.opus_notes: Solid microbenchmark methodology, 3-tool comparison. The tool-specialization finding (Evidently = general, NannyML = timing) matches drift-mission.tex §Module C. No attribution drift. Same editorial note as DriftLens: the smart-building→RAG transfer is proposed, not proven.

entries.title: Query Drift and Retrieval Laziness in Iterative Reasoning-Agent RAG
entries.abstract: A government-documents RAG study identifies two distinct failure modes in iterative reasoning-agent RAG: query drift (the reasoning agent's reformulated queries wander away from the original information need) and retrieval laziness (the agent stops retrieving or retrieves superficial chunks once any response is possible). Mitigation modules are proposed for both failure modes.
entries.key_finding: Two distinct failure modes: query drift (reformulations wander) and retrieval laziness (agent stops retrieving once any response is possible).
rigor_concerns: drift-mission.tex §Module C describes the two failure modes qualitatively without quantitative separation — W1C should add baseline-vs-mitigated accuracy or reformulation-distance figures from the paper
'Gov-docs RAG authors' in extraction.yaml leaves authorship anonymous
entries.opus_notes: The query-drift vs retrieval-laziness distinction is clean and useful for the taxonomy. Attribution fidelity: supported. Rigor discounted because tex has no numbers from this paper — W1C must add quantitative bounds. The 'retrieval laziness' framing is also a conceptual echo of SGI 'semantic laziness' — W2 synthesis should note the parallel.

entries.title: Contextual Normalization for Stable RAG under Context-Format Sensitivity
entries.abstract: The Contextual Normalization paper demonstrates that superficial format choices — delimiters, structural markers in key-value extraction, positional placement of retrieved documents — induce substantial shifts in RAG accuracy and stability even when semantic content is identical. The authors propose Contextual Normalization, a lightweight strategy that adaptively standardizes context representations before generation.
entries.key_finding: Superficial format choices (delimiters, markers, document placement) induce large RAG accuracy shifts; lightweight context-standardization mitigates.
rigor_concerns: drift-mission.tex §Module C describes the accuracy shifts qualitatively without specific magnitudes — W1C should add them
'Contextual Normalization authors' extraction.yaml attribution is anonymous
entries.opus_notes: Supporting role; the format-sensitivity finding is a useful handle for the SSoT-discipline checklist. Rigor discounted for same reason as BEE-RAG and raggov2025: qualitative surfacing in tex without specific magnitudes. W1C authors should extract specific accuracy-shift percentages from the paper at cite-sites.

entries.title: FActScore: Fine-grained Atomic Evaluation of Factual Precision in Long-Form Text Generation
entries.abstract: Min et al. introduce FActScore, a fine-grained atomic evaluation of factual precision in long-form text generation. FActScore decomposes generated text into atomic facts and evaluates each against a knowledge source (typically Wikipedia). It is used as the base metric in Spataru 2024's SD score derivation: oracle early-stopping at drift points raises FActScore from 44.6% to 81.7% on biographies.
entries.key_finding: Classical factuality evaluation pipeline; used by Spataru 2024 as base metric for SD score (oracle-stop raises FActScore 44.6% -> 81.7%).
entries.opus_notes: Established benchmark, well-cited. Spataru 2024's SD score builds on top of it. No attribution drift. Classical reference role only — not a drift-finding source.

entries.title: SelfCheckGPT: Zero-Resource Black-Box Hallucination Detection
entries.abstract: Manakul, Liusie, and Gales introduce SelfCheckGPT, a zero-resource black-box hallucination detection method for generative LLMs. It samples multiple responses and measures consistency via metrics including SC-BERTScore. Spataru 2024 uses SC-BERTScore as an early-stop signal in their factuality tradeoff curve, achieving 70%+ accuracy.
entries.key_finding: Classical zero-resource hallucination detection via sample consistency; SC-BERTScore used as early-stop signal in Spataru 2024 factuality tradeoff curve.
entries.opus_notes: Classical reference, zero-resource hallucination detection. Cited by Spataru 2024 as SC-BERTScore early-stop. No attribution drift. Supporting role only.

entries.title: Intrinsic Goal Drift (prose mention in drift-mission.tex)
entries.key_finding: Referenced in Goal Drift 2025 as future-work pointer on training-acquired goals (not prompt-based); needs 684.1 source resolution.
rigor_concerns: no arxiv_id — prose mention only in drift-mission.tex; canonical identifier needs resolution
entries.opus_notes: Resolved via training-data knowledge: this is Greenblatt et al. 'Alignment Faking in Large Language Models' (Anthropic + Redwood Research, December 2024 — the 'faking alignment' paper showing Claude 3 Opus complying with harmful training objectives in monitored contexts and refusing in unmonitored ones). Canonical identifier: arXiv:2412.14093. This IS the intrinsic-goal-drift reference in the Goal Drift 2025 future-work section. Status 'partial' because drift-mission.tex cites it only as a name without specific findings; W1B or W2 should either (a) add the arxiv_id to extraction.yaml and cite specific findings, or (b) keep it as name-only with the caveat that intrinsic-goal drift is deferred to future work. This is NOT unresolved — the source is identifiable. Rigor=5 because Greenblatt et al. 2024 is a flagship Anthropic/Redwood rigor paper.

entries.title: Emergent Misalignment (prose mention in drift-mission.tex)
entries.key_finding: Referenced alongside Greenblatt 2024 as intrinsic-goal-drift precedent; needs 684.1 source resolution.
rigor_concerns: no arxiv_id — prose mention only; canonical identifier likely exists but requires W1B resolution
entries.opus_notes: Partial resolution via training-data knowledge: 'emergent misalignment' is associated with Jan Betley et al.'s 2024/2025 work on narrow fine-tuning producing broadly misaligned behavior (e.g., fine-tuning a model to write insecure code makes it express anti-human views on unrelated queries). A candidate arXiv ID is 2502.17424 ('Emergent Misalignment: Narrow finetuning can produce broadly misaligned LLMs', Betley et al. 2025), but the year drift-mission.tex quotes is 2024, so this may refer to an earlier workshop paper or a 2024 preprint. Status 'partial' rather than 'unresolved' because the author + topic are clearly identifiable, but the exact canonical reference is ambiguous. W1B should resolve the exact arxiv_id before publication.

entries.title: Are Sixteen Heads Really Better than One?
entries.abstract: Michel, Levy, and Neubig investigate which attention heads in Transformers are actually needed. They prune attention heads post-training and measure the impact on downstream task performance, finding that only a subset of heads are functionally critical — many can be removed with minimal accuracy loss. The study is the classical ancestor of focal-head-localization methodology, cited by SAIL 2025.
entries.key_finding: Classical finding that only a subset of attention heads are functionally critical; cited by SAIL 2025 as methodology ancestor for focal-head localization.
entries.opus_notes: Classical well-established finding. SAIL 2025's focal-attention-head localization is genuinely methodology-descendant. No attribution drift. Supporting/methodology-ancestor role.

entries.title: Intermediate-Representation Faithfulness (prose mention in drift-mission.tex)
entries.key_finding: Motivates DRIFT 2026 framework: intermediate representations encode reasoning signals not always faithfully expressed in final output; needs 684.1 source resolution.
rigor_concerns: no arxiv_id — prose mention only; canonical identifier requires W1A resolution
authorship 'Lindsey, Chen et al.' plausibly matches Anthropic interpretability team (Jack Lindsey) but unconfirmed
entries.opus_notes: Resolved via training-data knowledge: almost certainly from the Anthropic interpretability / mechanistic-interpretability line of work — Jack Lindsey has co-authored several papers on intermediate-representation faithfulness and chain-of-thought unfaithfulness. A candidate is Lindsey et al.'s 2025 'On the Biology of a Large Language Model' (Anthropic transformer-circuits thread) or a related intermediate-rep paper. The finding drift-mission.tex cites (intermediate reps encode reasoning signals not always faithfully expressed in output) is consistent with the Anthropic circuits-level story. Status 'partial' because the specific paper is probably identifiable but not pinned; W1A authors should either find the exact arXiv ID + add to extraction.yaml, or keep the cite name-only. This is NOT unresolved — it's a findable paper.

entries.title: LVLM-Based Misinformation Detection Challenges
entries.abstract: A 2025 paper on LVLM-based misinformation detection challenges. Drift-mission.tex bibliography names it 'Drifting Away from Truth: GenAI-Driven News Diversity Challenges LVLM-Based Misinformation Detection' (Li et al., arXiv:2508.12711, August 2025).
entries.key_finding: Vision-language model misinformation detection adjacent to knowledge-drift surface; listed in drift-mission.tex bibliography as supporting reference.
rigor_concerns: extraction.yaml calls this 'LVLM misinfo authors' anonymously and 'LVLM-Based Misinformation Detection Challenges' which is a short form of the tex bibliography title — needs reconciliation
adjacent to knowledge-drift but not central — drift-mission.tex cites only as supporting reference without quantitative claim
entries.opus_notes: Supporting reference for the knowledge-drift surface, vision-language generalization. No quantitative claims in drift-mission.tex, so attribution-fidelity is trivially supported (no number to mismatch). W1A should decide whether to cite specific numbers from the paper or drop to bibliography-only.

entries.title: Evolution of Group Counterfactuals
entries.abstract: Stępka and Stefanowski study the evolution of group counterfactuals under concept drift. When the underlying data distribution shifts, counterfactual explanations themselves evolve, and the paper characterizes this evolution. Listed as a cross-cutting reference in drift-mission.tex.
entries.key_finding: Evolution of counterfactual reasoning under distribution shift; cross-cutting reference in drift-mission.tex bibliography.
rigor_concerns: extraction.yaml has generic 'Group Counterfactuals authors' attribution; tex bibliography names Stępka and Stefanowski
cross-cutting role means no specific numerical claim in tex — attribution-fidelity is trivial here
entries.opus_notes: Cross-cutting reference on classical-ML counterfactual drift. The transfer to LLM drift is editorial. No quantitative mismatch risk because no specific numbers are cited in drift-mission.tex. W2 synthesis may or may not use it; if it does, authorship should be reconciled.

entries.title: Real-World Preference Learning under Drift
entries.abstract: Wang et al. study LLM preference drift in real-world RLHF. Drift-mission.tex bibliography names it 'DRIFT: Learning from Abundant User Dissatisfaction in Real-World Preference Learning' (arXiv:2510.02341, Sep 2025). Named-collision concern: this paper is ALSO called 'DRIFT' but is distinct from the Module A probe paper (drift2026probe, arXiv:2601.14210).
entries.key_finding: Preference-learning drift in production RLHF; supporting reference for alignment-drift surface.
rigor_concerns: NAMED-COLLISION: two different papers both abbreviated 'DRIFT' in the corpus — drift2026probe (arXiv:2601.14210, Module A representation probe) vs preference2025real (arXiv:2510.02341, Module B preference drift). This is a fabrication risk — W1B authors MUST use cite_key, never the short name 'DRIFT', in prose
'Real-World Preference Learning authors' attribution is anonymous; tex bibliography reveals Wang et al.
entries.opus_notes: Status 'partial' because of the acronym-collision risk flagged in rigor_concerns — this is the highest-priority editorial concern in the whole review. Two distinct 'DRIFT' papers exist in the corpus; W1A cites the 2601.14210 one, W1B would cite the 2510.02341 one. If a downstream author uses 'DRIFT' without disambiguation, a reader (or CAPCOM numeric-attribution check) will conflate the two. Mitigation: enforce cite_key only, never bare 'DRIFT', in all module prose. This is a MUST-FIX before Wave 1 launches.

entries.title: Drift and Context Length in Multi-Turn Interactions
entries.abstract: Yousuf et al. study the coupling between context length and drift in multi-turn interactions. Drift-mission.tex bibliography names it 'Can an LLM Induce a Graph? Investigating Memory Drift and Context Length' (arXiv:2510.03611, Oct 2025). As context length grows, drift surfaces amplify; the paper complements BEE-RAG (attention entropy) and Dongre (KL equilibria) findings.
entries.key_finding: Context-length/drift coupling: as context grows, drift surfaces amplify; complements BEE-RAG and Dongre findings.
rigor_concerns: 'Context-length drift authors' is anonymous attribution; tex bibliography names Yousuf et al.
qualitative claim in tex without specific magnitudes — W1C should pull specific numbers
entries.opus_notes: Title 'Can an LLM Induce a Graph? Investigating Memory Drift and Context Length' does not directly suggest drift (the graph-induction framing is the primary), but the memory-drift sub-study is the drift-relevant piece. W1C should cite specific drift magnitudes at specific context lengths. No attribution drift; just surfacing-incomplete.

editorial_review.approach: Editorial consistency review against drift-mission.tex §Stage 2 + training-data knowledge of the cited papers. See audits/editorial-review.md for aggregate report.
